narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic performance and test scores to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.20 (below the typical 3.21 average), LSAT score of 22 (well under the 36.70 average), Year 1 school decile of 3 (versus an average of 5.71), and Year 3 decile of 1 (compared to 5.53)—and predicted a 63% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This high failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 academic decile, your LSAT score, and your Year 1 academic decile, ranked by their impact on pushing the prediction one way or the other. Most critically, your very low Year 3 decile ranking in your school—placing you in the bottom tier—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, as it signals weaker performance in later undergraduate years when habits solidify. Your LSAT score reinforced this concern, similarly driving the prediction higher toward failure risk, given how far it fell from what's typical for successful bar takers. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 3 worked in your favor, nudging the model slightly toward expecting a pass by showing somewhat stronger early performance relative to peers. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your later academic standing and test scores stand out as bigger drags than average, while your full-time enrollment status aligns with the vast majority (over 90%) and didn't sway things much. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or your dedication—many factors like family income played neutral roles here—but the model weighs these metrics heavily for bar success patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your growth from Year 1. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors at schools that value improvement stories like yours. Keep pushing; bar passage is achievable with targeted preparation, and many students overcome similar hurdles.
